Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic most of the time. I'd try to set aside time on weekends to watch videos and go through materials, but with limited resources at home, I often had to work around when I could access good internet at the library or a friend's place. I'd download what I could and then study offline during the week. It wasn't the most organized system, but I made it work with what I had.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was due and what else was going on in my life. I work part-time to support myself, so some weeks I barely had time to breathe, let alone study consistently. When deadlines approached, I'd cram everything into a few intense sessions. I wished I could be more regular, but life just doesn't allow for that kind of stability right now.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline looming or getting a notification about new content being posted. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there were any announcements I'd missed, because missing something important terrified me. The anxiety of falling behind was honestly a big motivator for logging in, even when I didn't feel like studying.
Q4: There was a period mid-semester when I got into a rhythm of checking the LMS every morning before work. It became almost automatic, like checking my messages. The gamified tasks they introduced actually helped with that—earning those small achievements gave me a reason to keep coming back even when motivation was low. But that routine fell apart when my work schedule changed.
Q5: The video lectures were everything for me since I'm a visual learner. I'd watch them multiple times, pausing to take screenshots of diagrams and important slides. The discussion boards were also helpful when I was confused because seeing other students' questions made me realize I wasn't the only one struggling with certain concepts. Those resources made the material actually stick.
Q6: I mostly ignored the supplementary readings and extra PDFs. With limited time and energy, I had to prioritize what would directly help me with assignments and exams. Some of those resources felt like nice-to-haves rather than essentials. I always told myself I'd go back to them, but I never did.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to my grades or if it helped clarify something I was confused about. The gamified tasks were surprisingly engaging because they gave immediate feedback and made me feel like I was accomplishing something. If something felt too abstract or disconnected from assessments, I'd skip it.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't afford most of the time. Required ones had this weight of consequence attached—I had to do them or face the anxiety of failing. Optional ones, even if interesting, got pushed aside because my mental bandwidth was already stretched thin with the essentials. It's not that I didn't want to explore more; I just couldn't.
Q9: Absolutely, a huge amount of my studying happened offline. I'd download videos and PDFs when I had good internet access, then study them on my phone or laptop without being connected. I also made handwritten notes from the visual materials because writing things down helps me remember. None of that showed up in the system.
Q10: There were entire weeks where I'd be reviewing my downloaded notes and rewatching saved videos, but the LMS would show zero activity from me. I'd be actively learning—highlighting, making flashcards, even explaining concepts out loud to myself—but digitally it looked like I'd disappeared. My offline study time probably doubled what the system recorded.
Q11: All the time. I'd watch a video once online, download it, and then rewatch it two or three more times offline while commuting or during breaks at work. Same with lecture slides—I'd access them once to download, then spend hours studying them on paper printouts. The LMS only saw that initial access.
Q12: There were definitely dark periods, especially around midterms when my anxiety spiked really badly. I felt scared that I wasn't keeping up, that everyone else understood things I didn't. My attendance was terrible because I was working to pay rent, and that made me feel even more behind. Those weeks were really hard to push through.
Q13: Constantly. When I was exhausted from work or stressed about money, the last thing I wanted to do was log into the LMS and face all those incomplete tasks. The fatigue made everything feel overwhelming. Sometimes I'd open the platform, see everything I needed to do, and just close it because I couldn't handle the anxiety it triggered.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd scroll through materials or have a video playing but my mind was completely elsewhere. I'd be worrying about bills or replaying stressful conversations in my head. Technically I was 'watching' the content, but I wasn't absorbing anything. Those sessions felt like wasted time afterward.
Q15: I postponed discussion posts a lot because they made me anxious. Putting my thoughts out there for others to see felt exposing, and I'd worry about sounding stupid. I knew participation mattered, but I'd keep telling myself I'd do it later when I felt more confident. That confidence rarely came.
Q16: Deadlines basically controlled my entire LMS usage. I'd have periods of low activity, then suddenly I'd be on constantly in the days before something was due. It wasn't healthy, but the pressure of deadlines was the only thing that could cut through my low motivation and anxiety. Without them, I probably would have fallen apart completely.
Q17: When I got good feedback on assignments, it gave me a temporary boost—like maybe I wasn't as lost as I thought. But negative or critical feedback would send me into a spiral where I'd avoid the course for days. The gamified elements helped because the feedback was more neutral, just progress indicators rather than judgments on my abilities.
Q18: The course structure was both helpful and stressful. Having clear modules and deadlines gave me something to work toward, which I needed. But the pace felt relentless sometimes, and with my low attendance, I was always playing catch-up. I wished there was more flexibility for students like me who can't always be present.
Q19: The gamified tasks actually made engagement easier because they broke things into smaller, manageable pieces. That helped with my anxiety a lot. But the heavy emphasis on live sessions and attendance made things harder since I couldn't always be there. More asynchronous options would have helped students in my situation.
Q20: I think those students might be like me—logging in frequently to check things, watching videos, exploring materials, but struggling to actually complete and submit work. The anxiety of submitting something imperfect can be paralyzing. Or they might be dealing with personal stuff that makes it hard to follow through even when they're trying to engage.
Q21: That actually describes me pretty well. My attendance and login frequency were inconsistent, but I was studying hard offline with downloaded materials. I got decent grades because I put in the work, just not always in ways the system could see. Performance doesn't always match what the platform records.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you're active, what you click on, how long you spend on things. But they miss the quality of that engagement and all the learning that happens away from the screen. They can't see if you're anxious, distracted, or deeply focused. They capture behavior, not understanding or effort.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about learning the material, not just going through the motions. It's when you're curious, when you think about the content even outside of study time, when you push through difficulties because you want to understand. For me, engagement was often fighting through anxiety and fear to keep showing up.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how scared I was most of the time, or how much effort it took just to open the platform some days. It wouldn't show the hours I spent studying offline, or the way the gamified tasks helped me feel less overwhelmed. It definitely wouldn't show that despite everything—the anxiety, the low resources, the work schedule—I was genuinely trying my best.
